ere, in response to numerous requests, is our revised version of ooks
 and  of the anarion along with   e ide , piphanius’ summary of the
catholic faith as he understood it.
 great deal need not be said by way of introduction. he tet from
which this is made is again oll’s, with notes completed after his death
by his grateful pupil ans ietzmann. e have used ummer’s reedition,
which includes various suggestions for the improvement of oll’s tet. or
piphanius’ life and work and our defense of him, the reader is referred to
the introduction to our ook , the second edition, rill, . he style of
ooks  and  is perhaps marginally better than that of ook  pipha-
nius quotes a number of better educated authors, some of his own writing
is formal, and he is discussing contemporary controversies with which he
was involved. owever, the same criticisms which apply to the rest of the
anarion, apply here.
he content is of particular interest to the patrologist, church historian,
theologian, student of nosticism or anichaeism, and the hristian with
theological interests, because it represents the hristian fourth century
as described by an active participant. olitically the church was trium-
phant and eercised considerable control over the lives of its people. he
monastic movement was new, on the rise and very important. nternally,
however, the church seethed with controversy, deathly serious, with all
parties convinced that the right answer was available in an infallible, self-
interpreting scripture, and that one’s eternal salvation depended upon
understanding it.
ecause piphanius was on the winning side we have the anarion
entire. ts comprehensiveness undoubtedly made it an important weapon
for the group which gained control of the church.
s the years between  and  were crucial to the rian problem
which the ouncil of icaea had failed to settle, this is given significant
space in the anarion’s ooks  and . ive long ects—or eight if
we count the brief notices of heodotianists, abellians and oetians—
deal with some aspect of it, a total of  pages out of . hree ects
deal with the date of aster—again, this was dealt with at icaea but the
 ee usebius’ fragmentary   e ascha ,  , ff., translated in trobel pp. –.

translator’s introduction
compromise it reached may not have been fully adopted when pipha-
nius wrote.   o ther topics prominent in the anarion are the divinity and
personality of the oly pirit, celibacy, ary’s perpetual virginity and the
resurrection of the body. ll these were hot issues in piphanius’ time and
account roughly for four fifths of ooks  and .
he longest ect is piphanius’ attack on the anichaeans, in his day
active and a serious competitor of the church. ts length, however, is due
in part to his fictitious biography of ani, in part to his paraphrase of
and partial quotation from the   cta rchelai disputationis cum anete
disputantis .
he quotation of other works is an important feature of the anarion.
here were several in ook  in ooks  and  there are no less than
fourteen, many not available elsewhere. n addition there are two self-
quotations a long passage from the ncoratus and piphanius’ etter to
rabia about ary.
s to his refutations of the various sects, piphanius takes these where
he finds them. ometimes we know the source his reply to oetus comes
from ippolytus, his strictures against the hrygians from one of several
possible sources. is own are not bad. is voice is most recognizable,
either in arguments drawn from simple commonsense—as when he asks
how ani’s archons can lock the soul in the prison of the body after eat-
ing it—or in his dealings with scripture. hese latter can be impressive.
is answers to rius’ arguments—barring a few forced eplanations—are
quite effective. ven his refutation of etius’ reatise on the ngenerate
and enerate, though it makes no real attempt to take issue with the dia-
lectic, is a reasonable hristian response.
lso of interest is the picture piphanius incidentally gives of the first
century church. t is interesting and important to know how oly eek
was kept, how a monk dressed, the not entirely successful attempts to
enforce clerical celibacy, the severe regulations concerning fasting.   o nly
piphanius eplains why a priest should wear a beard, or gives the names,
not of all but of several of the parish churches in leandria. o him also
we owe descriptions of some of the pagan celebrations he abhorred.
hile, read through, the anarion is monotonous and repetitious, some
passages show real imagination. hus, at the conclusion of his condem-
nation of the athari, piphanius, not unsympathetically, portrays the
position of the sectarian “t is  as though  one found a break in a wall
beside a highway, thought of going through it, left the road and turned off
 there , in the belief that a place where he could turn and pick up the
road again was right close by. ut he did not know that the wall was very

translator’s introduction   i
high and ran on for a long way () he kept running into it and not find-
ing a place to get out, and in fact went for more than a signpost, or mile,
further without reaching the road. nd so he would turn and keep going,
tiring himself out and finding no way to get back to his route and perhaps
he could never find one unless he went back to the place where he had
come in (,,–).” piphanius would have been an effective preacher.
his revision has been concerned chiefly with the translation and inde.
he translation has been carefully reviewed, its errors corrected, and it has
been tightened in the sense of being made more literal—not, we hope, at
the epense of readability. he notes have been enlarged, by adding a few
more lemmata, but chiefly by increasing the number of entries. s to the
notes themselves, these have only been minimally changed. rrors have
been corrected, a few more recent editions have been used, and the bib-
liography slightly updated. owever considerations of time preclude any
thorough revision of the notes.
lthough great care has been taken, there will still be errors the trans-
lator apologizes for them. perience has proved this translation helpful
to many. e hope it will continue to be so in the future.

ere likewise are the contents of this first ection of olume wo counted
consecutively from the beginning of the sections it is ection our. t con-
tains eighteen ects
. ncratites, who are an offshoot of atian, reject marriage and say
that it is of atan, and forbid the eating of any sort of meat.
. hrygians, also called ontanists and ascodrugians. hey accept
the ld and the ew estaments but, by boasting of a ontanus and a
riscilla, introduce other prophets after the canonical] prophets.
. () epuzians, also called uintillianists, with whom rtotyrites are
associated. hey derive from the hrygians but teach different doctrines.
hey venerate epuza, a deserted city somewhere in alatia, appadocia
and hrygia, and regard this as erusalem. (here is another epuza as
well.) nd they allow women to rule and to act as priests.
() heir initiation is the stabbing of a small child. nd they tell the
story that hrist was revealed in female form to uintilla, or riscilla,
there in epuza.
() hey likewise use the ld and the ew estaments, revising them
to suit their own taste.
. uartodecimans, who celebrate the assover on one day of the
year, whichever day is the fourteenth of the month—whether on a ab-
bath or a ord’s ay—and both fast and hold a vigil on that day.
. logi, or so  have named them, who reject the ospel of ohn and
the eternal divine ord in it who has (come down) from on high, from
the ather, and so accept neither ohn’s ospel itself, nor his evelation.
. () damians, by some called damizers, whose doctrine is not true
but ridiculous. () or they assemble stark naked, men and women alike,
and conduct their readings, prayers and everything else in that condition.
his is because they are supposedly single and continent and, since they
regard their church as aradise, do not allow marriage.
. ampsaeans, also called lkasaites, who live to this day in rabia,
the country lying north of the ead ea. hey have been deceived by
lai, a false prophet () whose descendants were arthus and arthana,
two women who are still worshipped as goddesses by the sect. ll their
doctrines are quite like those of the bionites.

anacephalaeosis iv
. heodotians, who derive from heodotus the shoemaker, of yzan-
tium. e ecelled in the reek education, but when he was arrested with
others during the persecution in his time, only he fell away. ecause he
was reproached after the martyrdom of the others, to escape the charge
of denying od he thought of the epedient of calling hrist a mere man,
and taught in this vein.
. elchizedekians, who honor elchizedek and claim he is a power
of some sort and not a mere man, and have dared to ascribe everything
to his name and say as much.
. ardesians. ardesianes came from esopotamia. t first he was
a follower of the true faith and ecelled in wisdom, but after he swerved
from the truth he taught like alentinus, ecept for a few small points
 in  which he differs from alentinus.
. () oetians. oetus was from myrna in sia. rom conceit he
taught, among other things, that hrist is the on-ather,  and said 
that the ather, the on and the oly pirit are the same. () e also said
that he was oses his brother, he said, was aron.
. () alesians. hey live,  believe, in the chief village of hiladelphia
in rabia, acathus they make eunuchs of all who happen by and accept
their hospitality. ost of them are castrated eunuchs themselves. () hey
teach certain other things which are full of heresy, reject  the teachings 
of the aw and the rophets, and introduce certain other obscenities.
. urists (athari), who are connected with avatus of ome, entirely
reject the twice-married, and do not accept repentance.
. ngelics. hese have entirely died out. ither they boasted of
angelic rank, or they were called ngelics  because they worshipped* 
angels.
. postolics, also called potactics. hese too  live  in isidia they
accept only persons who renounce the world, and they pray by them-
selves. hey are quite like the ncratites, but have opinions which are
different from theirs.
. abellians, whose opinions are like the oetians’ ecept that they
deny that the ather has suffered.
. rigenists, the disciples of one rigen. hey are obscene, have
unspeakable practices, and devote their bodies to corruption.

encratites   
. ther rigenists, the disciples of the rigen who is called daman-
tius the uthor. hey reject the resurrection of the dead, represent hrist
and the oly pirit as creatures, allegorize aradise, the heavens and all
the rest, and foolishly say that hrist’s kingdom will come to an end.
hese, in turn, are the eighteen ects of olume wo, ection ne.
 gainst ncratites.    umber , but  of the series
 , ertain persons whom we call ncratites are the successors of atian.
hey were led astray and deceived by atian in person, but have ideas dif-
ferent from his and in their own turn have devoted themselves to worse
foolishness. () ven today their numbers are increasing in isidia and
the land called corched hrygia. (erhaps the country has come to be
called this by divine dispensation, for this very reason—its inhabitants
have been scorched by the perversity of such error, and so much of it. or
there are many sects in the area.)
, here are also ncratites in sia, sauria, amphylia, ilicia and
alatia. nd by now this sect  has  also  been planted  in ome  to 
an etent, and at ntioch in yria as well—not everywhere, however.
, ncratites too say that there are certain sovereign authorities, and
that the  power  of the devil is ranged against od’s creatures because
the devil is not subject to od he has power of his own and acts as in his
own right, and not as though he had fallen into perversity. or they do
not agree with the church, but differ from its declaration of the truth.
, s scriptures they use principally the so-called cts of ndrew, and
of ohn, and of homas, and certain apocrypha, and any sayings from the
ld estament that they care to.
 piphanius may have used ren. aer. .., but clearly has contemporary knowl-
edge of the ncratites. ther ancient discussions are found at ippol. aer. . us. . .
.– lem. le. aedag. .. trom. .. .. ... he apocryphal cts of
ohn, ndrew and homas afford many instances of the sort of teachings described here.

 “et rulers be broken, let powers fall” is said of atan’s host at cts of ohn .
 he apocryphal cts represent the devil as a powerful, dangerous being at cts of
ndrew  cts of homas     . t cts of homas  the devil says, “he on
of od hath wronged me against my will, and taken them that were his own from me.”
 he ag ammadi tractate, homas the ontender ( , ) contains a sharp
polemic against seual intercourse, but there is no evidence that the “ncratites,” as
described here, used it.

encratites
, hey declare that marriage is plainly the work of the devil nd
they regard meat as an abomination—though they do not prohibit it for
the sake of continence or as a pious practice, but from fear and for appear-
